meta.title: TB-DF-5: Data Preparation, Core Analysis, and Sharing

questions.stem: A team is evaluating the next step. what is the main purpose of a context filter in Tableau?

options.text: It defines a primary filter that other filters can depend on
options.explanation: A context filter creates a temporary sub-table of the data that is used as the starting point for all other filters and computations in the view; this is especially important for making top-N filters and set conditions work correctly within a specified subset.
Workbook publishing is performed through the Server or File menu; it is not related to context filters.
Changing a field between measure and dimension roles is done by right-clicking the field in the Data pane; a context filter applies to an already-classified field.
Unions are created in the Data Source page by stacking tables; a context filter is a view-level filter setting applied to an existing connected source.

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. a JSON source contains nested attributes that need to be analyzed with relational tables later. What is the best first step inside Tableau Desktop?

options.text: Build a map immediately regardless of the fields
options.explanation: Tableau flattens JSON nested structures into columns when you connect; inspecting the resulting fields in the Data Source page first confirms which attributes were successfully parsed, their data types, and whether any restructuring (such as pivoting or renaming) is needed before building views.

options.text: Convert every field into a tooltip
options.explanation: Tooltips display supplementary information in a mark; converting every field to a tooltip before understanding the data structure is not a useful preparation step.

options.text: Connect to the JSON source and inspect the logical fields Tableau exposes before modeling downstream views
options.explanation: Publishing before reviewing the data source means distributing a workbook whose fields have not been validated; any structural issues will propagate to all consumers.

options.text: Publish the workbook before reviewing the data source
options.explanation: A map requires geographic fields (latitude, longitude, or recognized place-name fields); building a map immediately without reviewing which fields the JSON connector surfaced may produce errors or an empty view.

questions.stem: You need to choose the most appropriate action. which Marks card property controls the hue used for marks?

options.explanation: The Color property on the Marks card controls the hue, saturation, and opacity of marks; placing a dimension on Color assigns a distinct hue to each member, while placing a measure on Color applies a sequential or diverging color gradient.
The Detail property adds a field to increase mark granularity without encoding it visually as size, color, or label; it adds more individual marks without changing their color.
The Tooltip property makes a field available in the hover tooltip text; it does not affect the color or any other visual property of the marks.
The Rows shelf defines what field creates row headers or the vertical axis structure of the view; it is not a Marks card property and does not control mark color.

questions.reference: [Control the Appearance of  Marks in the View - Tableau](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/viewparts_marks_markproperties.htm)

questions.stem: Which Marks card property controls the hue used for marks?

options.explanation: The Color property on the Marks card controls the hue, saturation, and opacity of marks; placing a dimension on Color assigns a distinct hue to each member, while placing a measure on Color applies a sequential or diverging color gradient.
The Detail property adds a field to increase mark granularity without encoding it visually as size, color, or label; it adds more individual marks without changing their color.
The Tooltip property makes a field available in the hover tooltip text; it does not affect the color or any other visual property of the marks.
The Rows shelf defines what field creates row headers or the vertical axis structure of the view; it is not a Marks card property and does not control mark color.

questions.reference: [Control the Appearance of  Marks in the View - Tableau](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/viewparts_marks_markproperties.htm)

questions.stem: A team is evaluating the next step. what is a `.twbx` file used for in Tableau?

options.text: Using a packaged workbook that bundles the workbook with local data resources
options.explanation: A .twbx (Tableau Packaged Workbook) bundles the .twb workbook definition together with local data files (Excel, CSV, extracts) and image resources into a single archive, making it easy to share with recipients who do not have access to the original data sources.

options.text: Using a file that stores only project permissions
options.explanation: Project permissions are managed on Tableau Server or Cloud by administrators and are not stored in a workbook file.

options.text: Using a workbook version history archive on the server
options.explanation: Tableau Server maintains version history server-side; the .twbx format is a local file package, not a server-side version archive.
Model evaluation reports are a machine-learning concept; .twbx is a visualization packaging format specific to Tableau Desktop.

questions.stem: A field appears green in the view and creates a numeric axis. What does that most likely indicate?

options.text: It is being used as a continuous field
options.explanation: Tableau uses green to indicate continuous fields; a continuous field generates a quantitative axis that spans from the minimum to the maximum value rather than creating discrete headers for each value.

options.text: It is a published permission rule
options.explanation: Permission rules are administrative settings managed in Tableau Server or Cloud; they do not manifest as pill colors in a view.
Hidden worksheets are tabs that are not visible in presentation mode but still exist in the workbook; their hidden status is shown in the sheet tab row, not by pill color in the view.
Input tables are a Sigma Computing feature for writeback; they are not a Tableau concept, and no Tableau field type produces green pills to indicate input table status.

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. which TWO chart choices commonly fit geographic analysis in Tableau? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Symbol maps plot a proportionally sized mark (typically a circle) at each geographic location, making it easy to compare magnitude across places while retaining spatial context.
Filled maps shade geographic polygons (countries, states, postal codes) by a measure's value, allowing viewers to see regional patterns through color intensity across defined boundaries.
A union view is not a recognized Tableau chart type; a union is a data preparation operation that stacks rows, not a geographic visualization.
A join chart is not a Tableau visualization type; a join is a data source operation that combines columns from related tables, not a type of map or chart.

questions.reference: [Maps and Geographic Data Analysis in Tableau - Tableau](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/maps.htm)

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. which source type can Tableau Desktop connect to directly?

options.explanation: Tableau Desktop includes a built-in Excel connector that supports both .xls and .xlsx files and can read multiple sheets; Excel is one of the most commonly used direct connection types.

While Tableau extracts (.hyper or .tde) are supported, restricting Tableau's connectors to extracts alone is false; Tableau connects to dozens of live source types including databases, cloud services, and files.
Tableau connects to CSV through the Text File connector, but CSV is not the only supported source type; many other connectors exist.
Tableau Desktop connects to cloud warehouses, local files, on-premises databases, and many other source categories simultaneously; cloud warehouses are one subset of the available connectors.

questions.stem: An analyst needs each state's sales as a percent of the overall national total in the current table. Which approach best fits?

options.text: Use a percent-of-total table calculation or an equivalent calculation pattern
options.explanation: The Percent of Total quick table calculation (or an equivalent `SUM([Sales]) / WINDOW_SUM(SUM([Sales]))` formula) divides each state's SUM(Sales) by the total across all states in the current table partition, producing the desired share value for each row.

options.text: Replace the measure with a tooltip only
options.explanation: Placing a field only on the Tooltip shelf removes it from the view as a visible mark or axis; the analyst needs the percentage shown as a value in the table, not only as hidden hover text.
A union stacks rows from multiple tables in the Data Source page; it does not compute proportional shares of a total.

options.text: Convert the workbook into Tableau Reader
options.explanation: Tableau Reader is a free viewer application used to open packaged workbooks; converting to Reader format is a sharing method, not a calculation technique.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of an `IF` or `CASE` calculation in Tableau?

options.text: To return different results based on logical conditions
options.explanation: `IF` and `CASE` are conditional logic functions; an `IF` expression evaluates Boolean conditions in sequence and returns the value associated with the first true branch, while `CASE` matches a field's value against a list of options, both enabling branching logic within a calculated field.

options.text: To create a union between tables
options.explanation: Unions are created in the Data Source page by dragging or selecting additional tables to append; they are not produced by IF or CASE calculations.
Publishing extracts is an action performed through the Server or File menu to save a .hyper file to Tableau Server or Cloud; it is not a calculation type.
Project permissions are administrative settings managed in Tableau Server or Cloud by site administrators; they are not defined through workbook calculations.

questions.stem: Why can `FIXED` LOD results differ from a simple aggregate in the current view?

options.text: Because the LOD can compute at a different declared granularity than the visible marks
options.explanation: `FIXED [Customer] : SUM([Sales])` always computes the sum at the customer level regardless of whether the view is broken out by order date, region, or any other dimension; the view-level aggregation may be grouping the fixed values at a coarser grain, producing a different result from SUM([Sales]) which adapts to the current mark level of detail.

options.text: Because Tableau ignores the source data
options.explanation: FIXED LOD expressions query the same underlying source data as all other calculations; Tableau does not ignore source data when evaluating LODs.

options.text: Because LOD expressions are not aggregated
options.explanation: LOD expressions are aggregated—FIXED uses an explicit aggregate function (SUM, AVG, COUNT, etc.) within the expression; they are not unaggregated row-level values.
The data type returned by a FIXED LOD is determined by the aggregate function used; SUM and AVG return numeric types, not text.

questions.stem: A certification-style scenario describes how view should always show the top 10 products within the currently selected region. Which feature is most appropriate?

options.text: Using a dynamic top-N set or filter based on the current context
options.explanation: A top-N filter or set with a context filter on Region will recompute the top 10 products within whichever region is currently selected, making the ranking dynamic and responsive to user input without manual updates.
A static image export captures a fixed snapshot of the current view; it does not update when a different region is selected and provides no interactive filtering.

options.text: Using a union in the Data Source page
options.explanation: A union in the Data Source page stacks rows from multiple tables; it does not implement conditional top-N filtering in a view.
Changing measures to discrete changes their visual encoding (from axes to headers) but does not produce a top-N filter or make the ranking responsive to region selection.

questions.stem: A design review asks for justification. why should an analyst be cautious with packed bubbles or treemaps for exact comparisons?

options.text: Area-based encodings are harder to compare precisely than aligned lengths
options.explanation: Human perception judges area differences less accurately than length differences along a common baseline; viewers can reliably compare bar heights side by side but struggle to judge whether one circle or rectangle is 20% or 30% larger than another, making packed bubbles and treemaps poor choices when precise ranking or exact value comparison is required.
Packed bubbles and treemaps do display dimensions; the dimension field defines which group each bubble or rectangle represents.

options.text: They are the only option for quantitative data
options.explanation: Packed bubbles and treemaps are one option among many for quantitative data; bar charts, line charts, scatter plots, and other types are equally valid depending on the analytical goal.
Tooltips work on all Tableau mark types including circles and rectangles; packing bubbles or using a treemap does not disable tooltip display.

questions.reference: [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm)

questions.stem: A team is evaluating the next step. what does changing the aggregation from `SUM` to `AVG` do?

options.text: It changes how Tableau summarizes the measure in the current view
options.explanation: The aggregation setting determines how Tableau rolls up individual row values into a single mark value; switching from SUM to AVG means each mark now shows the arithmetic mean of the underlying records rather than their total, which can produce a very different result for the same dimension breakdown.
Republishing requires a deliberate publish action through the Server or File menu; changing an aggregation setting modifies only the calculation applied in the current view.

options.text: It changes a join into a union
options.explanation: Join and union are data preparation operations configured in the Data Source page; they are not affected by how a measure is aggregated in a view.

options.text: It converts a dimension into a hierarchy
options.explanation: Hierarchies are created from dimension fields by grouping them in the Data pane; aggregation functions are applied to measures and do not interact with dimension hierarchy creation.

questions.stem: A team needs to share a dashboard with offline users who do not have server access. Which option is most appropriate?

options.text: Distribute a packaged workbook for Tableau Reader when the use case fits
options.explanation: Tableau Reader is a free application that can open packaged workbooks (.twbx files) without requiring a Tableau Server or Cloud license; distributing the .twbx file allows offline users to view and interact with the dashboard using the bundled data.

options.text: Require every user to edit calculated fields manually
options.explanation: Requiring end users to edit calculated fields assumes they have Tableau Desktop licenses and analytical expertise; it does not address the requirement of offline access for non-technical stakeholders.

options.text: Convert the dashboard into a context filter
options.explanation: A context filter is a query-scoping feature within a worksheet; it cannot be converted into a shareable dashboard format.

options.text: Use only a live connection to a private database
options.explanation: A live connection to a private database requires network access to the database server; offline users by definition cannot reach private network resources.

questions.stem: What does a quick table calculation like running total do?

options.text: Applies a common table-calculation pattern without writing the full formula manually
options.explanation: Quick table calculations are pre-built calculation templates (Running Total, Percent of Total, Rank, Moving Average, etc.) that Tableau applies to an existing measure with a right-click, generating the correct RUNNING_SUM, WINDOW_SUM, or RANK function automatically without requiring the author to type the formula.

options.text: Publishes the workbook to the server
options.explanation: Publishing is a deliberate action performed through the Server menu; applying a quick table calculation modifies only the local view's computation without any server interaction.

options.text: Creates a data source filter automatically
options.explanation: A data source filter restricts which rows are included before any aggregation; it is set in the Filters pane of the Data Source page, not by applying a table calculation to a measure.
Quick table calculations apply to an existing measure without affecting any other fields; they do not reclassify dimensions as measures or alter the Data pane.

questions.stem: What does changing the aggregation from `SUM` to `AVG` do?

options.text: It changes how Tableau summarizes the measure in the current view
options.explanation: The aggregation setting determines how Tableau rolls up individual row values into a single mark value; switching from SUM to AVG means each mark now shows the arithmetic mean of the underlying records rather than their total, which can produce a very different result for the same dimension breakdown.
Republishing requires a deliberate publish action through the Server or File menu; changing an aggregation setting modifies only the calculation applied in the current view.

options.text: It changes a join into a union
options.explanation: Join and union are data preparation operations configured in the Data Source page; they are not affected by how a measure is aggregated in a view.

options.text: It converts a dimension into a hierarchy
options.explanation: Hierarchies are created from dimension fields by grouping them in the Data pane; aggregation functions are applied to measures and do not interact with dimension hierarchy creation.

questions.stem: A team is evaluating the next step. what is the purpose of an `IF` or `CASE` calculation in Tableau?

options.text: To return different results based on logical conditions
options.explanation: `IF` and `CASE` are conditional logic functions; an `IF` expression evaluates Boolean conditions in sequence and returns the value associated with the first true branch, while `CASE` matches a field's value against a list of options, both enabling branching logic within a calculated field.

options.text: To create a union between tables
options.explanation: Unions are created in the Data Source page by dragging or selecting additional tables to append; they are not produced by IF or CASE calculations.
Publishing extracts is an action performed through the Server or File menu to save a .hyper file to Tableau Server or Cloud; it is not a calculation type.
Project permissions are administrative settings managed in Tableau Server or Cloud by site administrators; they are not defined through workbook calculations.

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. a team is evaluating the next step. what is the main purpose of Data Interpreter in Tableau?

options.text: It cleans common Excel formatting issues such as title rows and footers during import
options.explanation: Subscription scheduling is a server-side feature in Tableau Cloud and Tableau Server that sends email snapshots to users; it is not a Data Interpreter capability.

options.text: It creates level of detail expressions
options.explanation: Data Interpreter analyzes spreadsheets at import and removes common formatting artifacts—such as merged header cells, title rows, and footer text—that would otherwise appear as data rows, producing a cleaner field structure for analysis.

options.text: It publishes extracts to Tableau Cloud automatically
options.explanation: Publishing extracts to Tableau Cloud is a separate step performed through the Publish menu after connecting to and preparing data; Data Interpreter is an import-time cleaning tool.
Level of detail (LOD) expressions are custom calculations written by the author; they are not generated by Data Interpreter.

questions.stem: What is the most important governance reason to publish a workbook into the correct project?

options.text: The project can carry appropriate permission and content-management settings
options.explanation: Projects in Tableau Server and Cloud act as permission containers; a workbook published to the correct project inherits that project's default permissions, content certification policies, and governance settings, ensuring the right audience has the right level of access.

options.text: It changes every chart into a map
options.explanation: Project placement has no effect on visualization types; charts remain whatever type the workbook author built regardless of which project the workbook lives in.

options.text: It removes the need for data source credentials
options.explanation: Data source credentials are managed at the connection level through saved credentials or connection settings; the project a workbook belongs to does not affect authentication to the underlying data source.
Project location does not affect query execution performance; performance is determined by the data source, extract freshness, and the complexity of the workbook's calculations.

questions.stem: A team is evaluating the next step. what is the main purpose of Data Interpreter in Tableau?

options.explanation: Subscription scheduling is a server-side feature in Tableau Cloud and Tableau Server that sends email snapshots to users; it is not a Data Interpreter capability.

options.text: It cleans common Excel formatting issues such as title rows and footers during import
options.explanation: Data Interpreter analyzes spreadsheets at import and removes common formatting artifacts—such as merged header cells, title rows, and footer text—that would otherwise appear as data rows, producing a cleaner field structure for analysis.

options.text: It publishes extracts to Tableau Cloud automatically
options.explanation: Publishing extracts to Tableau Cloud is a separate step performed through the Publish menu after connecting to and preparing data; Data Interpreter is an import-time cleaning tool.

options.text: It creates level of detail expressions
options.explanation: Level of detail (LOD) expressions are custom calculations written by the author; they are not generated by Data Interpreter.

questions.stem: A practitioner wants to solve the following problem. when is a dual-axis chart most appropriate?

options.explanation: A dual-axis chart overlays two measures on independent left and right axes in the same view, which is useful when the measures have very different scales (e.g., Revenue in millions and Units Sold in thousands) or when different mark types (bar and line) are combined to contrast trends.

options.text: When data needs to be unioned
options.explanation: Workbook certification is a governance endorsement applied in Tableau Server or Cloud; it is not a criterion for choosing a dual-axis chart type.

options.text: When two measures need to be compared in the same view, often with different mark types or scales that are handled carefully
options.explanation: A union stacks rows from multiple tables in the Data Source page; it is a data preparation operation completely separate from dual-axis chart design.

options.text: When a workbook must be certified
options.explanation: Dual-axis charts require at least two measures to create both axes; a view with only dimension fields has no quantitative values to plot on independent axes.

questions.reference: [Add Axes for Multiple Measures in Views - Tableau](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/multiple_measures.htm)

questions.stem: A certification-style scenario describes how JSON source contains nested attributes that need to be analyzed with relational tables later. What is the best first step inside Tableau Desktop?

options.text: Connect to the JSON source and inspect the logical fields Tableau exposes before modeling downstream views
options.explanation: Tableau flattens JSON nested structures into columns when you connect; inspecting the resulting fields in the Data Source page first confirms which attributes were successfully parsed, their data types, and whether any restructuring (such as pivoting or renaming) is needed before building views.

options.text: Convert every field into a tooltip
options.explanation: Tooltips display supplementary information in a mark; converting every field to a tooltip before understanding the data structure is not a useful preparation step.

options.text: Publish the workbook before reviewing the data source
options.explanation: Publishing before reviewing the data source means distributing a workbook whose fields have not been validated; any structural issues will propagate to all consumers.

options.text: Build a map immediately regardless of the fields
options.explanation: A map requires geographic fields (latitude, longitude, or recognized place-name fields); building a map immediately without reviewing which fields the JSON connector surfaced may produce errors or an empty view.

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. a candidate is reviewing certification objectives. which view is often best for showing correlation between two quantitative measures?

options.explanation: A scatter plot places one measure on the horizontal axis and a second on the vertical axis, with each mark representing an individual data point; the resulting point cloud reveals the direction, strength, and form of the relationship between the two measures.

options.text: Stacked bar chart of one category only
options.explanation: A stacked bar chart visualizes part-to-whole composition within a single categorical dimension; it does not plot two measures against each other in a way that reveals correlation.
A text table without measures contains no quantitative values to compare; it cannot communicate correlation between numeric variables.
A hierarchy control (the expand/collapse drill-down interface) is a navigation feature, not a chart type; it does not display data points in any layout that reveals correlation.

questions.reference: [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm)

questions.stem: What is a calculated field in Tableau?

options.text: A user-defined expression that creates a new field from existing data
options.explanation: A calculated field is a new virtual column defined by a formula (using functions, operators, and existing fields) that Tableau evaluates at query time; it behaves like any other field and can be placed on shelves, used in filters, and referenced in other calculations.

options.text: A permission rule on a project
options.explanation: Permission rules are administrative settings in Tableau Server or Cloud that control user access; they are not formula-based fields.

options.text: A type of workbook export only
options.explanation: Workbook exports (PDF, image, CSV) are output formats; a calculated field is a persistent data definition, not a one-time export.
Static image annotations are fixed text or visual elements added to a view for context; they are not computed from data.

questions.stem: A team is evaluating the next step. what is a common reason to use a live connection instead of an extract?

options.text: To query the underlying source directly for the latest available data
options.explanation: A live connection sends each query directly to the source database, so the results always reflect the most current data without requiring a scheduled extract refresh.

options.text: To make the workbook unusable online
options.explanation: Live connections are fully supported in Tableau Cloud and Tableau Server; using a live connection does not impair online access.
Filters work the same way whether the connection is live or an extract; live connections do not disable any filter functionality.

Live connections introduce latency from the round-trip query to the source database, especially for large datasets; performance trade-offs still exist and may be more significant than with a local extract.

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes how analyst needs each state's sales as a percent of the overall national total in the current table. Which approach best fits?

options.text: Use a percent-of-total table calculation or an equivalent calculation pattern
options.explanation: The Percent of Total quick table calculation (or an equivalent `SUM([Sales]) / WINDOW_SUM(SUM([Sales]))` formula) divides each state's SUM(Sales) by the total across all states in the current table partition, producing the desired share value for each row.

options.text: Replace the measure with a tooltip only
options.explanation: Placing a field only on the Tooltip shelf removes it from the view as a visible mark or axis; the analyst needs the percentage shown as a value in the table, not only as hidden hover text.
A union stacks rows from multiple tables in the Data Source page; it does not compute proportional shares of a total.

options.text: Convert the workbook into Tableau Reader
options.explanation: Tableau Reader is a free viewer application used to open packaged workbooks; converting to Reader format is a sharing method, not a calculation technique.

questions.stem: A team is evaluating the next step. what does sorting a dimension field change in a worksheet?

options.text: The order in which the members appear
options.explanation: Sorting a dimension changes the sequence in which its members are rendered—alphabetical, by measure value ascending or descending, manually, or by nested sort—affecting only the display order, not the underlying data.
Publishing is a separate action performed through the Server or File menu; sorting a dimension does not trigger or affect publication.
The data source is determined by the connection configured in the Data Source page; applying a sort to a dimension does not change where the data comes from.
Project permissions in Tableau Server or Cloud are managed by site administrators; they are not modified by how a dimension is sorted in a worksheet.

questions.stem: An analytics team is reviewing its next step. what does disaggregating data usually change in a Tableau view?

options.explanation: When you deselect Aggregate Measures in the Analysis menu, Tableau removes the default aggregation and plots a separate mark for each individual data row rather than a single aggregated mark per dimension combination, which is useful for scatter plot analysis of raw values or for examining data distribution.
Disaggregation is a view-rendering option that changes how marks are computed; it has no effect on the data source connection or its files.

options.text: It can show underlying records instead of summarized marks
options.explanation: Filters continue to operate normally in disaggregated mode; they restrict which rows are included before marks are plotted.
Disaggregation changes the aggregation behavior of measures, not the continuous or discrete classification of fields; fields retain their existing type assignments.

questions.stem: Which TWO chart choices commonly fit geographic analysis in Tableau? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Symbol maps plot a proportionally sized mark (typically a circle) at each geographic location, making it easy to compare magnitude across places while retaining spatial context.
Filled maps shade geographic polygons (countries, states, postal codes) by a measure's value, allowing viewers to see regional patterns through color intensity across defined boundaries.
A union view is not a recognized Tableau chart type; a union is a data preparation operation that stacks rows, not a geographic visualization.
A join chart is not a Tableau visualization type; a join is a data source operation that combines columns from related tables, not a type of map or chart.

questions.reference: [Maps and Geographic Data Analysis in Tableau - Tableau](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/maps.htm)

questions.stem: How does a set differ from a group in Tableau?

options.text: A set defines membership that can be dynamic or conditional, while a group combines members into named categories
options.explanation: A set partitions members of a dimension into an In group and an Out group based on fixed selection, a condition, or a top-N rule that can update dynamically as the data changes, while a group manually combines selected members into a new named category that behaves as an additional dimension value.

options.text: A set is only for maps, while a group is only for tables
options.explanation: Both sets and groups can be created from any dimension regardless of the visualization type; neither is restricted to maps or tables.

options.text: A set is always numeric, while a group is always date-based
options.explanation: Sets and groups are created from any dimension type, including string, date, or numeric; neither is restricted to a specific data type.
Sets and groups serve meaningfully different purposes: sets drive In/Out logic usable in calculations and actions, while groups create new fixed category labels.

questions.stem: Which shelf on the Marks card controls whether text labels appear on marks?

options.explanation: Placing a field on the Label shelf of the Marks card adds that field's value as text displayed directly on or near each mark; the Label shelf also provides options to control label alignment, font, and visibility conditions.
The Pages shelf creates a page-by-page animation or flip book of the view broken out by the values of the field placed there; it controls animated playback, not text labeling.
The Filters shelf restricts which rows of data are included in the view; it does not control text annotation on marks.
The Columns shelf determines what fields define the horizontal axis or column headers; it is a view structure shelf, not a mark-level labeling control.

questions.reference: [Control the Appearance of Marks in the View](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/viewparts_marks_markproperties.htm)

questions.stem: What does disaggregating data usually change in a Tableau view?

options.text: It can show underlying records instead of summarized marks
options.explanation: When you deselect Aggregate Measures in the Analysis menu, Tableau removes the default aggregation and plots a separate mark for each individual data row rather than a single aggregated mark per dimension combination, which is useful for scatter plot analysis of raw values or for examining data distribution.
Disaggregation is a view-rendering option that changes how marks are computed; it has no effect on the data source connection or its files.
Filters continue to operate normally in disaggregated mode; they restrict which rows are included before marks are plotted.
Disaggregation changes the aggregation behavior of measures, not the continuous or discrete classification of fields; fields retain their existing type assignments.

questions.stem: A candidate is reviewing certification objectives. which source type can Tableau Desktop connect to directly?

options.explanation: Tableau Desktop includes a built-in Excel connector that supports both .xls and .xlsx files and can read multiple sheets; Excel is one of the most commonly used direct connection types.

While Tableau extracts (.hyper or .tde) are supported, restricting Tableau's connectors to extracts alone is false; Tableau connects to dozens of live source types including databases, cloud services, and files.
Tableau connects to CSV through the Text File connector, but CSV is not the only supported source type; many other connectors exist.
Tableau Desktop connects to cloud warehouses, local files, on-premises databases, and many other source categories simultaneously; cloud warehouses are one subset of the available connectors.

questions.stem: Consider the following requirement. what is a common use case for a highlight table?

options.explanation: A highlight table is a text table where cells are colored by a measure's value, making it easy to scan rows and columns simultaneously and spot high and low values, outliers, or patterns across two categorical breakdowns.
Cross-database joins are configured in the Data Source page canvas; they are a data preparation task unrelated to chart type selection.
Subscriptions are a server-side delivery feature; they schedule email snapshots of views and are not related to highlight tables.

options.text: Showing magnitude patterns across rows and columns with color encoding
options.explanation: Cleaning spreadsheet headers is an import-time task handled by Data Interpreter or manual field renaming in the Data Source page; it is not a function of any chart type.

questions.reference: [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm)

questions.stem: Why might an analyst choose an extract instead of a live connection?

options.text: To use a snapshot or potentially improve performance for some use cases
options.explanation: An extract stores a columnar .hyper file optimized for Tableau's query engine; for large datasets or slow source databases this can significantly reduce query latency, and the snapshot enables analysis when the source system is unavailable.

options.text: To remove all need for refreshes forever
options.explanation: Extracts must be refreshed—manually or on a schedule—to incorporate new source data; they do not eliminate the need for data updates.
An extract captures the data at a point in time; it is the opposite of real-time and will not reflect source changes until refreshed.
All calculation types—including LOD expressions, table calculations, and row-level formulas—work the same way on extracts as on live connections.

questions.stem: A candidate is reviewing certification objectives. which Marks card property controls the hue used for marks?

options.explanation: The Color property on the Marks card controls the hue, saturation, and opacity of marks; placing a dimension on Color assigns a distinct hue to each member, while placing a measure on Color applies a sequential or diverging color gradient.
The Detail property adds a field to increase mark granularity without encoding it visually as size, color, or label; it adds more individual marks without changing their color.
The Tooltip property makes a field available in the hover tooltip text; it does not affect the color or any other visual property of the marks.
The Rows shelf defines what field creates row headers or the vertical axis structure of the view; it is not a Marks card property and does not control mark color.

questions.reference: [Control the Appearance of  Marks in the View - Tableau](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/viewparts_marks_markproperties.htm)

questions.stem: When would a pivot in the Data Source page be most appropriate?

options.text: When wide month columns need to become attribute-value rows
options.explanation: Pivoting in Tableau's Data Source page reshapes wide data—such as one column per month—into a long format with a single attribute column (Month) and a value column (Sales), which is the structure Tableau's shelf-based analysis expects.

options.text: When two database tables need an inner join
options.explanation: Joining tables uses the join editor in the Data Source page canvas, not the pivot feature; inner join is a join type that keeps only matching rows.

options.text: When a workbook must be published
options.explanation: Publishing is a server action performed through the File or Server menu after the workbook is built; it does not involve pivoting.

options.text: When a continuous field should become discrete
options.explanation: Changing a field from continuous to discrete is done by right-clicking the field pill in a view or in the Data pane; it is not performed through the Data Source pivot feature.

questions.stem: A design review asks for justification. why publish a data source separately from a workbook?

options.text: To let multiple workbooks reuse a governed source definition
options.explanation: A published data source on Tableau Server or Cloud is a shared, versioned connection definition that multiple workbooks can reference; this means credentials, joins, and calculated fields are maintained in one place and all connected workbooks benefit from updates automatically.

options.text: To prevent anyone from connecting to data
options.explanation: Publishing a data source makes it more accessible to authorized users, not less; restricting access is handled through permissions, not through the publishing action itself.
A published data source can use either a live connection or an extract; publishing does not disable extract functionality.
Field type assignments (measure vs. dimension) are set within the data source and are not changed by publishing; the assignment persists as defined.

questions.stem: A certification-style scenario describes how field appears green in the view and creates a numeric axis. What does that most likely indicate?

options.text: It is being used as a continuous field
options.explanation: Tableau uses green to indicate continuous fields; a continuous field generates a quantitative axis that spans from the minimum to the maximum value rather than creating discrete headers for each value.

options.text: It is a published permission rule
options.explanation: Permission rules are administrative settings managed in Tableau Server or Cloud; they do not manifest as pill colors in a view.
Hidden worksheets are tabs that are not visible in presentation mode but still exist in the workbook; their hidden status is shown in the sheet tab row, not by pill color in the view.
Input tables are a Sigma Computing feature for writeback; they are not a Tableau concept, and no Tableau field type produces green pills to indicate input table status.

questions.stem: A team is evaluating the next step. what is the main benefit of a context filter before a computationally expensive top-N filter?

options.text: It can reduce the data considered by the later top-N logic
options.explanation: A context filter materializes a temporary sub-table; when the top-N filter evaluates next, it operates only over the rows that passed the context filter rather than scanning the full dataset, which can significantly reduce query time and produce the intended scoped top-N result.
A context filter reduces the data scope but still generates a query to the data source; query cost is reduced, not eliminated.
Context filters work alongside other filters, not instead of them; other dimension and measure filters continue to apply on top of the context-filtered sub-table.

options.text: It converts live data into an extract automatically
options.explanation: A context filter is a query-planning instruction that affects how Tableau processes the current view; it does not change the connection type or create a persistent extract file.

questions.stem: What is a `.twbx` file used for in Tableau?

options.text: A packaged workbook that bundles the workbook with local data resources
options.explanation: A .twbx (Tableau Packaged Workbook) bundles the .twb workbook definition together with local data files (Excel, CSV, extracts) and image resources into a single archive, making it easy to share with recipients who do not have access to the original data sources.

options.text: A file that stores only project permissions
options.explanation: Project permissions are managed on Tableau Server or Cloud by administrators and are not stored in a workbook file.

options.text: A workbook version history archive on the server
options.explanation: Tableau Server maintains version history server-side; the .twbx format is a local file package, not a server-side version archive.
Model evaluation reports are a machine-learning concept; .twbx is a visualization packaging format specific to Tableau Desktop.

questions.stem: An exam scenario describes the following situation. what is the visual difference between discrete and continuous fields in Tableau?

options.explanation: Placing a discrete field on a shelf produces a header row or column for each distinct member value, while placing a continuous field produces a numeric axis that spans from a minimum to a maximum value, reflecting the field's range rather than its individual members.

options.text: Discrete fields create headers, while continuous fields typically create axes
options.explanation: Sorting is available for both discrete and continuous fields; you can sort discrete dimension members and sort continuous measures independently.
Continuous fields can be filtered using range filters (slider-based min/max) or special filters; filtering is not restricted to discrete fields.
The difference is functionally significant: discrete fields produce headers and segmented color palettes, while continuous fields produce axes and sequential color gradients.

questions.stem: A team is evaluating the next step. what is the most important governance reason to publish a workbook into the correct project?

options.text: The project can carry appropriate permission and content-management settings
options.explanation: Projects in Tableau Server and Cloud act as permission containers; a workbook published to the correct project inherits that project's default permissions, content certification policies, and governance settings, ensuring the right audience has the right level of access.

options.text: It changes every chart into a map
options.explanation: Project placement has no effect on visualization types; charts remain whatever type the workbook author built regardless of which project the workbook lives in.

options.text: It removes the need for data source credentials
options.explanation: Data source credentials are managed at the connection level through saved credentials or connection settings; the project a workbook belongs to does not affect authentication to the underlying data source.
Project location does not affect query execution performance; performance is determined by the data source, extract freshness, and the complexity of the workbook's calculations.

questions.stem: A candidate is reviewing certification objectives. which chart type is most suitable for showing change in a measure over time?

options.explanation: A line chart encodes time on the horizontal axis and the measure on the vertical axis, with connected line segments that make trends, seasonality, and rate of change immediately visible to the viewer.
A treemap uses nested rectangles sized by a measure to show part-to-whole relationships; it has no inherent time axis and does not communicate change over time effectively.
Packed bubbles encode magnitude as circle area without a temporal axis; without a time dimension, they cannot show how a measure changes over time.

A highlight table uses color intensity in a matrix to show magnitude across row and column dimensions; while it can include a date dimension, it is optimized for cross-dimensional comparison, not for communicating trend direction.

questions.reference: [Choose the Right Chart Type for Your Data](https://help.tableau.com/current/pro/desktop/en-us/what_chart_example.htm)
